findings.justification: The entire unsanitized $_REQUEST['args'] array is passed directly to a dynamic do_action hook, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary data into any registered action handler that processes those args.

The $output captured from ob_get_clean() is emitted via die() without any escaping or wp_kses filtering (the TODO on line 66 confirms this is known), enabling XSS if any hooked action writes unescaped user-controlled content.

The $status parameter is interpolated directly into the transient key ("um_count_users_{$status}") without sanitization or length-capping, so a caller supplying an attacker-controlled $status string can poison arbitrary transient slots or cause collisions.
These user-enumeration functions contain no authorization check (no current_user_can()), so any authenticated or unauthenticated caller that can reach them can count users and infer account statuses.
The meta value comparison uses loose equality (==) instead of strict (===), which can lead to type-juggling bypasses when comparing numeric or boolean meta values.

The function passes the caller-supplied $key and $value directly into get_posts() as 'meta_key' and 'meta_value' without sanitization; while WP_Query parameterizes these, the deprecated 'meta_key'/'meta_value' flat args are a known source of SQL injection vectors in older WordPress versions.
